Label14.name: Label14
Label14.label: •	Determine if the design was correctly transferred.

Label9.name: Label9
Label9.label: •	Select a single design project (note: if the project selected involves a device that contains software, consider reviewing the software's validation while proceeding through the assessment of the firm's design control system).

Label23.name: Label23
Label23.label: •	For the design project selected, verify that design control procedures that address the regulatory requirements (e.g. FDA 21 CFR 820.30) have been defined and documented.

Label22.name: Label22
Label22.label: •	Review the design plan for the selected project to understand the layout of the design and development activities including assigned responsibilities and interfaces (note: evaluate the firm's conduct of risk analysis while proceeding through the assessment of the firm's Design Control system).

Label21.name: Label21
Label21.label: •	Confirm that design inputs were established.

Label19.name: Label19
Label19.label: •	Verify that the design outputs that are essential for the proper functioning of the device were identified.

Label20.name: Label20
Label20.label: •	Confirm that acceptance criteria were established prior to the performance of verification and validation activities.

Label18.name: Label18
Label18.label: •	Determine if design verification confirmed that design outputs met the design input requirements.

Label17.name: Label17
Label17.label: •	Confirm that design validation data show that the approved design met the predetermined user needs and intended uses.

Label16.name: Label16
Label16.label: •	Confirm that the completed design validation did not leave any unresolved discrepancies.

Label15.name: Label15
Label15.label: •	If the device contains software, confirm that the software was validated.

Label13.name: Label13
Label13.label: •	Confirm that risk analysis was performed.

Label10.name: Label10
Label10.label: •	Determine if design validation was accomplished using initial production devices or their equivalents.

Label11.name: Label11
Label11.label: •	Confirm that changes were controlled including validation or where appropriate verification.

Label12.name: Label12
Label12.label: •	Determine if design reviews were conducted.

Label1.name: Label1
Label1.label: •	Verify that a quality policy, management review and quality audit procedures, quality plan, and quality system procedures and instructions have been defined and documented.

Label2.name: Label2
Label2.label: •	Verify that a quality policy and objectives have been implemented.

Label3.name: Label3
Label3.label: •	Review the firm's established organizational structure to confirm that it includes provisions for responsibilities, authorities and necessary resources.

Label4.name: Label4
Label4.label: •	Confirm that a management representative has been appointed. Evaluate the purview of the management representative.

Label5.name: Label5
Label5.label: •	Verify that management reviews, including a review of the suitability and effectiveness of the quality system, are being conducted.

Label6.name: Label6
Label6.label: •	Verify that quality audits, including re-audits of deficient matters, of the quality system are being conducted.

Label7.name: Label7
Label7.label: •	At the conclusion of the inspection, evaluate whether management with executive responsibility ensures that an adequate and effective quality system has been established and maintained.

Label29.name: Label29
Label29.label: •	Determine if information regarding nonconforming product and quality problems and corrective and preventive actions has been properly disseminated, including dissemination for management review.

Label28.name: Label28
Label28.label: •	Verify that CAPA system procedure(s) that address the requirements of the quality system regulation have been defined and documented.

Label32.name: Label32
Label32.label: •	Determine if appropriate sources of product and quality problems have been identified. Confirm that data from these sources are analyzed to identify existing product and quality problems that may require corrective action.

Label31.name: Label31
Label31.label: •	Determine if sources of product and quality information that may show unfavorable trends have been identified. Confirm that data from these sources are analyzed to identify potential product and quality problems that may require preventive action.

Label27.name: Label27
Label27.label: •	Challenge the quality data information system. Verify that the data received by the CAPA system are complete, accurate and timely.

Label30.name: Label30
Label30.label: •	Verify that appropriate statistical methods are employed (where necessary) to detect recurring quality problems. Determine if results of analyses are compared across different data sources to identify and develop the extent of product and quality problems.

Label26.name: Label26
Label26.label: •	Determine if failure investigation procedures are followed. Determine if the degree to which a quality problem or nonconforming product is investigated is commensurate with the significance and risk of the nonconformity. Determine if failure investigations are conducted to determine root cause (where possible). Verify that there is control for preventing distribution of nonconforming product.

Label25.name: Label25
Label25.label: •	Determine if appropriate actions have been taken for significant product and quality problems identified from data sources.

Label24.name: Label24
Label24.label: •	Determine if corrective and preventive actions were effective and verified or validated prior to implementation. Confirm that corrective and preventive actions do not adversely affect the finished device.

Label8.name: Label8
Label8.label: •	Verify that corrective and preventive actions for product and quality problems were implemented and documented.

Label33.name: Label33
Label33.label: •	Verify that the firm has MDR procedures that address the requirements in 21 CFR Part 803.17.

Label34.name: Label34
Label34.label: •	Verify that the firm has established and maintains MDR event files that comply with 21 CFR Part 803.18.

Label36.name: Label36
Label36.label: •	Confirm that the appropriate MDR information is being identified, reviewed, reported, documented and filed.

Label35.name: Label35
Label35.label: •	Confirm that the firm follows their procedures and they are effective in identifying MDR reportable deaths, serious injuries and malfunctions.

Label37.name: Label37
Label37.label: •	Determine if corrections or removals of a device were initiated by the manufacturer.

Label39.name: Label39
Label39.label: •	Confirm that the firm's management has implemented the reporting requirements of 21 CFR Part 806.

Label38.name: Label38
Label38.label: •	Verify that the firm has established and continues to maintain a file for all non-reportable corrections and removals per 21 CFR Part 806.20. Also verify that the firm is complying with the other file-related requirements of 21 CFR Part 806.

Label40.name: Label40
Label40.label: •	Determine if the firm manufactures or imports a tracked device.

Label42.name: Label42
Label42.label: •	Verify that the firm has established a written standard operating procedure (SOP) for tracking that complies with the requirements in 21 CFR Part 821.25(c).

Label41.name: Label41
Label41.label: •	Verify that the firm’s quality assurance program includes audits of its tracking system within the appropriate time- frames specified in 21 CFR Part 821.25(c)(3).

Label43.name: Label43
Label43.label: •	Select a process for review based on:

Label49.name: Label49
Label49.label: o	CAPA indicators of process problems;

Label48.name: Label48
Label48.label: o	Use of the process for manufacturing higher risk devices;

Label45.name: Label45
Label45.label: o	Degree of risk of the process to cause device failures;

Label47.name: Label47
Label47.label: o	The firm’s lack of familiarity and experience with the process;

Label46.name: Label46
Label46.label: o	Use of the process in manufacturing multiple devices;

Label54.name: Label54
Label54.label: o	Variety in process technologies and profile classes;

Label55.name: Label55
Label55.label: o	Variety in process technologies and profile classes;

Label53.name: Label53
Label53.label: o	Any other appropriate criterion as dictated by the assignment

Label52.name: Label52
Label52.label: •	Review the specific procedure(s) for the manufacturing process selected and the methods for controlling and monitoring the process. Verify that the process is controlled and monitored (note: control and monitoring procedures may include in-process and or finished device acceptance activities as well as environmental and contamination control measures).

Label56.name: Label56
Label56.label: •	If review of the Device History Records (including process control and monitoring records, etc.) reveals that the process is outside the firm’s tolerance for operating parameters and/or rejects or that product nonconformances exist:

Label59.name: Label59
Label59.label: o	Determine whether any nonconformances were handled appropriately;

Label58.name: Label58
Label58.label: o	Review the equipment adjustment, calibration and maintenance;

Label57.name: Label57
Label57.label: o	Evaluate the validation study in full to determine whether the process has been adequately validated.

Label51.name: Label51
Label51.label: •	If the results of the process reviewed cannot be fully verified, confirm that the process was validated by reviewing the validation study.

Label44.name: Label44
Label44.label: •	If the process is software controlled, confirm that the software was validated.

Label50.name: Label50
Label50.label: •	Verify that personnel have been appropriately qualified to implement validated processes or appropriately trained to implement processes which yield results that can be fully verified.

Label66.name: Label66
Label66.label: •	Confirm that the sterilization process was validated by reviewing the validation study.

Label65.name: Label65
Label65.label: •	Review the specific procedure(s) for the sterilization process selected and the methods for controlling and monitoring the process. Verify that the process is controlled and monitored.

Label64.name: Label64
Label64.label: •	If review of the Device History Records (including process control and monitoring records, acceptance activity records, etc.) reveals that the sterilization process is outside the firm’s tolerance for operating or performance parameters:

Label63.name: Label63
Label63.label: o	Determine whether the nonconformances were handled appropriately; and

Label62.name: Label62
Label62.label: o	Review the equipment adjustment, calibration and maintenance

Label61.name: Label61
Label61.label: •	If the sterilization process is software controlled, confirm that the software was validated.

Label60.name: Label60
Label60.label: •	Verify that personnel have been appropriately qualified and trained to implement the sterilization process.

RichTextBox.name: RichTextBox
RichTextBox.label: What is the reason for this inspection

Label67.name: Label67
Label67.label: There can be risks associated with the following:

Label68.name: Label68
Label68.label: • planning, e.g. failure to set relevant inspection objectives and determine the extent, number, duration, locations and schedule of the inspections;

Label69.name: Label69
Label69.label: • resources, e.g. allowing insufficient time, equipment and/or training for developing the inspection program or conducting an inspection;

Label70.name: Label70
Label70.label: • selection of the inspection team, e.g. insufficient overall competence to conduct inspections effectively;

Label71.name: Label71
Label71.label: • communication, e.g. ineffective external/internal communication processes/channels;

Label72.name: Label72
Label72.label: • implementation, e.g. ineffective coordination of the inspections within the inspection program, or not considering information security and confidentiality;

Label73.name: Label73
Label73.label: • control of documented information, e.g. ineffective determination of the necessary documented information required by inspectionors and relevant interested parties, failure to adequately protect inspection records to demonstrate inspection program effectiveness

Label74.name: Label74
Label74.label: • monitoring, reviewing and improving the inspection program, e.g. ineffective monitoring of inspection program outcomes;

Label75.name: Label75
Label75.label: • availability and cooperation of inspectionee and availability of evidence to be sampled.

Label76.name: Label76
Label76.label: Opportunities for improving the inspection program can include:

Label77.name: Label77
Label77.label: • allowing multiple inspections to be conducted in a single visit;

Label78.name: Label78
Label78.label: • minimizing time and distances travelling to site;

Label79.name: Label79
Label79.label: • matching the level of competence of the inspection team to the level of competence needed to achieve the inspection objectives;

Label80.name: Label80
Label80.label: • aligning inspection dates with the availability of inspectionee’s key staff.

Label82.name: Label82
Label82.label: Warning: By selecting an inspection definition, all the fields in the form will be updated according to the selected inspection definition

Label81.name: Label81
Label81.label: The inspection client should ensure that the inspection program objectives are established to direct the planning and conducting of inspections and should ensure the inspection program is implemented effectively. Inspection program objectives should be consistent with the inspection client’s strategic direction and support management system policy and objectives.

ExampleObjectives.name: ExampleObjectives
ExampleObjectives.label: Examples of inspection objectives can include the following:

Availabilityofinformation.name: Availabilityofinformation
Availabilityofinformation.label: The availability of information and communication technologies (e.g. technical resources required to set up a remote audit using technologies that support remote collaboration)

Availabilityofanytoolstechnologyandequip.name: Availabilityofanytoolstechnologyandequipmentrequired
Availabilityofanytoolstechnologyandequip.label: The availability of any tools, technology and equipment required

Requirementsrelatedtothefacility.label: Requirements related to the facility, including any security clearances and equipment (e.g. background checks, personal protective equipment, ability to wear clean room attire)

Establishing extent of the inspection

The inspection client should ensure that the inspection program objectives are established to direct the planning and conducting of inspections and should ensure the inspection program is implemented effectively. Inspection program objectives should be consistent with the inspection client’s strategic direction and support management system policy and objectives.
Examples of inspection objectives can include the following:
•	Verify that a quality policy, management review and quality audit procedures, quality plan, and quality system procedures and instructions have been defined and documented.
•	Verify that a quality policy and objectives have been implemented.
•	Review the firm's established organizational structure to confirm that it includes provisions for responsibilities, authorities and necessary resources.
•	Confirm that a management representative has been appointed. Evaluate the purview of the management representative.
•	Verify that management reviews, including a review of the suitability and effectiveness of the quality system, are being conducted.
•	Verify that quality audits, including re-audits of deficient matters, of the quality system are being conducted.
•	At the conclusion of the inspection, evaluate whether management with executive responsibility ensures that an adequate and effective quality system has been established and maintained.
•	Determine if the design was correctly transferred.

•	Select a single design project (note: if the project selected involves a device that contains software, consider reviewing the software's validation while proceeding through the assessment of the firm's design control system).
•	For the design project selected, verify that design control procedures that address the regulatory requirements (e.g. FDA 21 CFR 820.30) have been defined and documented.

•	Review the design plan for the selected project to understand the layout of the design and development activities including assigned responsibilities and interfaces (note: evaluate the firm's conduct of risk analysis while proceeding through the assessment of the firm's Design Control system).
•	Confirm that design inputs were established.
•	Verify that the design outputs that are essential for the proper functioning of the device were identified.
•	Confirm that acceptance criteria were established prior to the performance of verification and validation activities.
•	Determine if design verification confirmed that design outputs met the design input requirements.
•	Confirm that design validation data show that the approved design met the predetermined user needs and intended uses.
•	Confirm that the completed design validation did not leave any unresolved discrepancies.
•	If the device contains software, confirm that the software was validated.
•	Confirm that risk analysis was performed.
•	Determine if design validation was accomplished using initial production devices or their equivalents.
•	Confirm that changes were controlled including validation or where appropriate verification.
•	Determine if design reviews were conducted.
•	Determine if information regarding nonconforming product and quality problems and corrective and preventive actions has been properly disseminated, including dissemination for management review.
•	Verify that CAPA system procedure(s) that address the requirements of the quality system regulation have been defined and documented.

•	Determine if appropriate sources of product and quality problems have been identified. Confirm that data from these sources are analyzed to identify existing product and quality problems that may require corrective action.

•	Determine if sources of product and quality information that may show unfavorable trends have been identified. Confirm that data from these sources are analyzed to identify potential product and quality problems that may require preventive action.
•	Challenge the quality data information system. Verify that the data received by the CAPA system are complete, accurate and timely.

•	Verify that appropriate statistical methods are employed (where necessary) to detect recurring quality problems. Determine if results of analyses are compared across different data sources to identify and develop the extent of product and quality problems.

•	Determine if failure investigation procedures are followed. Determine if the degree to which a quality problem or nonconforming product is investigated is commensurate with the significance and risk of the nonconformity. Determine if failure investigations are conducted to determine root cause (where possible). Verify that there is control for preventing distribution of nonconforming product.
•	Determine if appropriate actions have been taken for significant product and quality problems identified from data sources.

•	Determine if corrective and preventive actions were effective and verified or validated prior to implementation. Confirm that corrective and preventive actions do not adversely affect the finished device.
•	Verify that corrective and preventive actions for product and quality problems were implemented and documented.
•	Verify that the firm has MDR procedures that address the requirements in 21 CFR Part 803.17.
•	Verify that the firm has established and maintains MDR event files that comply with 21 CFR Part 803.18.
•	Confirm that the appropriate MDR information is being identified, reviewed, reported, documented and filed.
•	Confirm that the firm follows their procedures and they are effective in identifying MDR reportable deaths, serious injuries and malfunctions.
•	Determine if corrections or removals of a device were initiated by the manufacturer.
•	Confirm that the firm's management has implemented the reporting requirements of 21 CFR Part 806.

•	Verify that the firm has established and continues to maintain a file for all non-reportable corrections and removals per 21 CFR Part 806.20. Also verify that the firm is complying with the other file-related requirements of 21 CFR Part 806.
•	Determine if the firm manufactures or imports a tracked device.
•	Verify that the firm has established a written standard operating procedure (SOP) for tracking that complies with the requirements in 21 CFR Part 821.25(c).
•	Verify that the firm’s quality assurance program includes audits of its tracking system within the appropriate time- frames specified in 21 CFR Part 821.25(c)(3).
•	Select a process for review based on:
o	CAPA indicators of process problems;
o	Use of the process for manufacturing higher risk devices;
o	Degree of risk of the process to cause device failures;
o	The firm’s lack of familiarity and experience with the process;
o	Use of the process in manufacturing multiple devices;
o	Variety in process technologies and profile classes;
o	Variety in process technologies and profile classes;
o	Any other appropriate criterion as dictated by the assignment

•	Review the specific procedure(s) for the manufacturing process selected and the methods for controlling and monitoring the process. Verify that the process is controlled and monitored (note: control and monitoring procedures may include in-process and or finished device acceptance activities as well as environmental and contamination control measures).

•	If review of the Device History Records (including process control and monitoring records, etc.) reveals that the process is outside the firm’s tolerance for operating parameters and/or rejects or that product nonconformances exist:
o	Determine whether any nonconformances were handled appropriately;
o	Review the equipment adjustment, calibration and maintenance;
o	Evaluate the validation study in full to determine whether the process has been adequately validated.
•	If the results of the process reviewed cannot be fully verified, confirm that the process was validated by reviewing the validation study.
•	If the process is software controlled, confirm that the software was validated.
•	Verify that personnel have been appropriately qualified to implement validated processes or appropriately trained to implement processes which yield results that can be fully verified.
•	Confirm that the sterilization process was validated by reviewing the validation study.
•	Review the specific procedure(s) for the sterilization process selected and the methods for controlling and monitoring the process. Verify that the process is controlled and monitored.

•	If review of the Device History Records (including process control and monitoring records, acceptance activity records, etc.) reveals that the sterilization process is outside the firm’s tolerance for operating or performance parameters:
o	Determine whether the nonconformances were handled appropriately; and
o	Review the equipment adjustment, calibration and maintenance
•	If the sterilization process is software controlled, confirm that the software was validated.
•	Verify that personnel have been appropriately qualified and trained to implement the sterilization process.
Objectives
Scope
There can be risks associated with the following:
• planning, e.g. failure to set relevant inspection objectives and determine the extent, number, duration, locations and schedule of the inspections;
• resources, e.g. allowing insufficient time, equipment and/or training for developing the inspection program or conducting an inspection;
• selection of the inspection team, e.g. insufficient overall competence to conduct inspections effectively;
• communication, e.g. ineffective external/internal communication processes/channels;
• implementation, e.g. ineffective coordination of the inspections within the inspection program, or not considering information security and confidentiality;

• control of documented information, e.g. ineffective determination of the necessary documented information required by inspectionors and relevant interested parties, failure to adequately protect inspection records to demonstrate inspection program effectiveness
• monitoring, reviewing and improving the inspection program, e.g. ineffective monitoring of inspection program outcomes;
• availability and cooperation of inspectionee and availability of evidence to be sampled.
Risk
Opportunities for improving the inspection program can include:
• allowing multiple inspections to be conducted in a single visit;
• minimizing time and distances travelling to site;
• matching the level of competence of the inspection team to the level of competence needed to achieve the inspection objectives;
• aligning inspection dates with the availability of inspectionee’s key staff.
Opportunities
Determining inspection resources
When determining resources for the inspection, the individual(s) managing the inspectio should consider
Impact of different Time zones?
The availability of information and communication technologies (e.g. technical resources required to set up a remote audit using technologies that support remote collaboration)
The availability of any tools, technology and equipment required
Requirements related to the facility, including any security clearances and equipment (e.g. background checks, personal protective equipment, ability to wear clean room attire)
Inspection reporting output as required and to whom it is to be distributed matters related to confidentiality and information security
Any health, safety, and environmental arrangements for the inspectors?
Requirements for travel or access to remote sites
Any security and authorization requirements
